Kind of like Teslaphoresis?

author: @soumyyaresinspvtltd5142
text:
So i tried doing exactly this, and the results that i got were actually less strength than what they generally get. I was extremely disappointed by the results. So i tried making 2 batches with different blender, the results did have a difference in between the 2 tests, but the test results were actually derogatory to what they use without adding graphene

Secondly, i thought maybe my carbon source was not pure enough, then i bought sample of graphite flakes from a well known mines here in India with purity of carbon more than 99%,
i again made 2 batches with different blenders, and then i poured 12  6inches cubes with 4 different proportions of both the batches, and then ran a 7day test, trust me the results were showing lesser strength than what they usually get

FYI i tried on M35 grade design mix in which i added graphene at different proportions - ranging from 0.02% to 0.04% of cement by weight

Can you please share your design mix for the concrete that you made and tested and how much graphene did you add 

Also if you can guide where i could have been going wrong

Cheers and Regards! You are doing amazing work!

author: @jamesross1003
text: This is great,  add the graphene slurry in it to make it stronger.  We also have found out that you can graphene coat sand and add that as an aggregate to concrete to make it stronger.  So, my question is what happens if you do both?  Will that make it even stronger?  It makes sense that it might do so, because graphene wants to bond with itself.  That is why you used the process that you did in order to make it disperse without clumping back together.  So, would that make the dispersed in liquid graphene want to make a very strong bond with that which is coating your aggregate?  If I had a kiln capable of producing graphene coated sand,  I think that I would give this a go to find out.  Thanks for the video Rob!  I find these types of videos very interesting and entertaining.  Keep up the good work Rob!

author: @ScottAuch
text:
COMPARED TO STANDARD CONCRETE: adding as little as 0.02% graphene into concrete “can” make it up to 35x stronger, 146% increase in compressive strength and 79.5% in flexural strength, while decreasing water permeability by nearly 400%. At the same time also creating enhanced electrical and thermal performance. 

But keep in mind “Performance of the concrete is relative to the application.”

WHILE SEEMINGLY SIMPLE — just adding graphene to cement — there’s a whole chemistry aspect that needs to be carefully considered! 

Additives, mix ratios, ambient temperatures, mould dimensions, all play critical roles in the outcome quality. 

Through a lot of legwork and experience, Robert makes everything seem so simple, but in reality they are very complex theories and science. 

MEGA PROPS AND THANKS TO ROBERT!!!

- - - 

GRAPHENE AERATED (FOAMED) CONCRETE: has similar increased performance, but the methods used for designing normal concrete compositions CANNOT be used for aerated concrete. They are completely different (like AirCrete is actually foamed mortar).

author: @garethdavies4351
text:
Out of interest what cement class and mix design did you use for your cubes? 

There are 27 common cements in 197-1 standard ranging from CEM I containing no more than 5% additions up to CEM III/C that. Can be up to 95% additions (GGBS in this case and 5% only 5% cement clinker) 

Virtually no ready mix concrete in the UK is produced with a 32,5 strength cement

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: off the shelf ready mix portland blue circle is what i used

replies.author: @garethdavies4351
replies.text: Ah ok CEM II A/L 32,5R 👍 thank you I will get some tests done with a CEM I 42,5 and CEM I 52,5 (more common in ready-mix and precast applications when I have some time 😊

replies.author: @gshingles
replies.text: I don't think you'd want slidey molecular bonded material floating about in the mix. It seems to me that if concretion occurs around a bit of graphene it's going to resist movement very well, but if it forms around graphite, the graphite is going to easily slide apart under tension or stress, depending on the orientation of the stress to the planes of the graphite.

author: @theoriginalrecycler
text:
In respect of the cements where was the pozzolana?. Cements are the binders, aggregates provide the strength. Adding any ultra fine material to a concrete will reduce the amount of cement needed merely because there are fewer voids. 
Interestingly Wimpy tried to reduce the cost of concrete production by introducing “ no fines” concrete in the post war period. 
This was coarse aggregate with cement binder resulting in a clinker block. Successful strong matrices are the result of careful aggregate grading to ensure maximal packing and appropriate choice of cement. Ref AJ Neville properties of concrete.

replies.text: indeed - cheers

replies.author: @sailingoctopus1
replies.text: Rebar gives concrete the tensile strength it would otherwise lack; it appears that Graphene only increases concrete's compressive strength. Frankly, concrete has more than enough compressive strength for most applications, so I don't see graphene being a major step forward, except perhaps in aircrete and a few niche applications.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't know but bulding codes are hard to get around mate

replies.author: @ScottAuch
replies.text: Per application, it could provide a reduction in rebar use, but keep in mind “Performance of the concrete is relative to the application.”

replies.author: @rapturebound197
replies.text: @mikegLXIVMM  I don't know how or if the graphene enhanced cement changes anything in regard to the regs on rebar requirements. Logically, it would make sense if it did. My thought was if adding graphene to concrete reduces the amount of concrete needed, could graphene in steel produce similar results and have the same effect? The answer is no simply because it can not be added to steel. However, graphene can be added to enhance the coatings for steel used in concrete, thereby greatly enhancing the corrosion protection of the steel. Its a big win all around! The company I'm watching to make the biggest splash in graphene is Universal Matters. Their flash jouele process of making graphene was discovered and developed by a lab at Rice University by professor James Tour and his students.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
Thank you so much for revisiting this topic.  This method is easy enough I can play with it, and as I'm remodeling my grandmothers hundred year old house for my little family, this will help a lot.
And I think this with things like fiber reinforced thin film concrete will be Really cool, and I cannot wait to see if it can make aircrete more viable- as the bubbles kill the strength, but if you can strengthen the concrete matrix Around the bubbles, it may make up for that to some degree (I want to try to both put it in the concrete part,  and try it in the Foam part to see if maybe graphene coated bubbles inside to give those voids some more structure to see if either works better.  It will take some playing with, but that's where my mind goes.  This may be a cool way to further reduce the amount of concrete you have to use)

author: @alexd7466
text:
I want to try this with epoxy. Any tips on how to get the water out? 
Or better: instead of water, could this be mixed directly with resin in the blender? (without the hardener, ofc.)  The bubbles might be a problem though.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text:
I suspect not, but don't let me stop you. Vid 1933 explains how and why it was made this way which might show you why resin won't work, or it might give you hints how you CAN make it work. 
If you can centrifuge it though, you'd have the dry graphene to add to the resin and I'm sure that would work great.

replies.author: @hubrisnaut
replies.text: In his video on making it he says use a centrifuge otherwise it takes months to settle out. Either way, then pour off the water and spread it out to dry.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text:
One option to investigate: This wet mixture contains both graphene and left-over graphite. It may be that you'll get extra strength by including both without having to seperate them. So it's worth trying just evaporating the water-off powdering it again (it'll probably be  kinda stuck together) and seeing what happens. 
But yeah, otherwise it will most likely need centrifuging.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: centrifuge and dry

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
I had a semi-educated "MAGAt Moran" who was trying to tell me that Concrete is Carbon Neutral, because of its curing process.
His statement was, "The Calcium Carbonate loses it CO2 when burnt, but gains it back while curing."
And I asked, "But what are we burning it with? Wood, oil, coal, Natural Gas? Are THOSE Carbon Neutral flames?"
"Oh, YoU jUsT dOn'T wAnT tO lIsTeN tO rEaSoN!"
Proof that "A little knowledge is a dangerous thing."
And so is a closed mind.

replies.author: @welcomesp
replies.text: Really, when you thing about it, there is nothing that is carbon neutral....even us doing activity with our physical body.

replies.author: @MorganRoutledge
replies.text: Let's see here, you've come up with a derogatory term for people whom have an opposing pollical view to yourself and then try to paint them all of them using a single interaction with one of them that, no doubt, has been paraphrased to your benefit. The thing that really got me was calling him closed minded while being proudly bigoted towards him. This lack of ability to self-reflect is surprising common among the left and you see it in just about every comment section.

replies.author: @jay90374
replies.text: @welcomesp Exactly, when someone starts preaching about "carbon neutral", I tune completely out! 🤦🤡🤡🤡

replies.author: @weedfreer
replies.text:
​​​​@MorganRoutledge wrong use of the word bigoted.

The OP stated that they think less of the person they were describing for a perceived lack of intelligence.  The person described saw the limited part of a situation which rang true with his agenda while missing the bigger picture.

The OP may have had personal knowledge of this person's politics...and, so, his generalisation may well have actually been a statement of fact...he may also have experienced similar/consistent levels of interaction with other people he was aware are also in support of the same political ideas.

The OP, on the other hand, did not make a statement regards to his own politics.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text:
I actually wonder if lime mortar still absorbs so much CO2 ( the critical process for long-term strength increase and bonding) as it used to 100 years ago . We are now at 420 ppm CO2 in the atmosphere. Up from say 280 ppm . In a dry climate it might improve things. But in a damp foggy type climate like Britain and northwest USA, I'm starting to have questions. I'm 70 and have some observations . Co2 dissolves in moisture to yield carbonic acid , a too acid medium suppresses the formation of the calcium carbonate or it dissolves it . 
 And yes we can be cynical about some claims on being carbon neutral in the wider perspective view.

replies.author: @LiveWire937
replies.text: there's the hitch of it all: getting land. found a beautiful heel of a property a few months ago on the market for "$98,000." Steep grade, but that didn't bother me; nowhere to park, but I don't drive a car; 3 acres of forest, and I'd be going under as much as over so most of the trees would remain undisturbed... but it was in a historic area (whatever that means, anyway--there was a mobile home park just up the way a bit) and a 100 year flood plane... plus a few other land surveys and feasibility studies the city wanted me to pay for *before even letting my buy the property* and in the end, it would have cost me well over a million dollars by the time I ever got a foundation in. I'm trying to build a cottage here, not a mansion @_@
